design_philosophy.core_loop: Helicopter combat and resource management: pilot a combat helicopter through multi-objective missions balancing fuel consumption, armor depletion, ammunition limits, and mission objectives (destroy targets, rescue hostages, capture enemies)
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource scarcity vs. mission complexity—player must manage limited fuel, armor, and ammunition while navigating enemy-dense environments and completing multiple objectives per level
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Elite military helicopter pilot conducting covert strike operations across North American locations, mixing aerial combat with tactical ground infiltration
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Hybrid action-strategy design that refuses to be purely twitch-based; forces deliberate tactical choices (weapon selection, fuel management, landing zone planning) alongside execution skill. On-foot missions break helicopter gameplay rhythm, emphasizing varied gameplay over mastery of single mechanic

difficulty.lives_system: Player loses a life when armor depletes to zero or fuel runs out; helicopter destroyed upon life loss
difficulty.checkpointing: Mission-based structure; levels consist of multiple sequential missions within campaigns across North American locations
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Players select weapon loadouts (machine guns, Hydra rockets, Hellfire missiles) based on anticipated mission needs, creating tactical difficulty scaling. Trade-off between firepower and ammunition capacity requires strategic planning

progression.upgrade_system: Vehicle switching system: Mohican (primary, speed/combat-focused), Blackhawke (heavy lift, rescue-focused), experimental Ground Assault Vehicle (GAV). On-foot missions with rifle and missile launcher introduce gameplay variation within linear campaign
progression.unlock_mechanism: Campaign progression through 10 massive missions spanning Hawaii → Mexico → San Francisco → New York → Las Vegas, narrative-driven by antagonist H.R. Malone's superweapon construction
replayability_hooks: Weapon loadout experimentation (limited ammo creates different playstyle constraints)
Vehicle variety across levels (GAV in level 3, Blackhawke in early levels)
Multiple rescue/capture objectives with positional variation

world_building_techniques: Plot-driven campaign structure: protagonist discovers enemy Ego killed by car-bomb, escalates through geographic locations revealing antagonist's superweapon construction
Character revelation: plastic surgeon subplot reveals H.R. Malone is actually Carlos Ortega (presumed dead from Jungle Strike), building player connection to previous game's narrative
Mission briefings establish objective context within larger antagonist plan
Geographic progression (Hawaii → continental US → Las Vegas) mirrors real-world geopolitical thriller aesthetics

notable_bosses: H.R. Malone (final confrontation): millionaire media mogul antagonist defeated by player dropping him onto his own superweapon as it detonates

faction_indicators: H.R. Malone's organization (antagonist faction, populist appeal)
Military/government structures implied through mission structure
danger_signals: Malone's political influence despite election loss; agenda masks threat (appeals to legitimate concerns: violence, corruption, organized crime, while planning government destabilization)
Superweapon deployment against civilian centers (New York City mass destruction)
Penetration of Strike C.O.R.E. by Malone's agents (Agent Ego assassination)
environmental_storytelling: Mission locations reflect Malone's infrastructure (Hawaii mirror collection sites, Las Vegas superweapon facility)
Rescued civilians and POWs indicate contested territories and civilian impact
Destroyed installations (enemy weapons caches, military facilities) visualize resistance operations
